title: Obligations & Conditionals — なければ / ば / たら / なら / と

meta.focus: Express necessity with なければいけない and ないといけない, and contrast four conditional forms — ば (hypothetical), と (automatic result), たら (completed-event trigger), and なら (contextual). Also covers ば〜ほど for 'the more ~, the more ~'.

sections.title: Obligations & Conditionals
sections.summary: Japanese expresses obligation with two interchangeable patterns — なければいけない and ないといけない — both meaning 'must' or 'have to'. For conditions, Japanese uses four distinct forms: ば for general hypotheticals, と for automatic or inevitable results, たら for conditions triggered when an event completes, and なら for responses to context already established in conversation. Finally, the ば〜ほど pattern expresses 'the more ~, the more ~'.
sections.whyItMatters: These patterns are essential for natural Japanese conversation. You'll use なければいけない constantly to talk about obligations, and you'll hear all four conditional forms every day — each carrying a different shade of meaning. Mastering the distinctions between them is one of the biggest steps toward sounding natural at the N4 level.
youWillLearn: なければいけない and ないといけない — expressing 'must' or 'have to'
ば conditional — general 'if' for hypothetical conditions
と conditional — automatic or inevitable result ('when X, Y always follows')
たら conditional — condition triggered when an action is completed ('when X is done')
なら conditional — contextual 'if', responding to something already in the conversation
ば〜ほど — 'the more ~, the more ~' for degree escalation

sections.explanation: なければいけない is the standard obligation construction: 'must do ~'. It's formed by taking a verb's negative stem (the same stem used for plain_negative) and replacing ない with なければ, then adding いけない or なりません. The alternative ないといけない uses the plain negative + と + いけない and carries the same meaning with a slightly more casual feel.
notes: Formation (なければ form): Godan verbs — same stem as plain_negative (u→a) + なければ: 帰る→帰らなければ, 飲む→飲まなければ. Ichidan verbs — drop る + なければ: 食べる→食べなければ. Irregular: する→しなければ, くる→来なければ.
Politeness: casual = なければいけない; formal = なければなりません. Both express obligation.
Alternative form: ないといけない (plain_negative + と + いけない). 食べないと いけない = 食べなければ いけない. ないといけない is slightly more conversational.
In very casual speech, なければ contracts to なきゃ: 帰らなきゃ (I gotta go home). This contraction is common but not formally introduced until later.
いけない in obligation constructions is the plain negative of the verb いける. なりません is the polite negative of なる.

parts.gloss: go home (なければ form of 帰る — godan: 帰ら + なければ)
must — obligation marker (plain negative of いける)

examples.en: I must go home early today.
examples.breakdown: 帰る (godan, -ru) → u_to_a stem 帰ら + なければ = 帰らなければ. Full construction: 帰らなければ いけない = must go home.

parts.gloss: drink (なければ form of 飲む — godan: 飲ま + なければ)
must — polite obligation marker (polite negative of なる)

examples.breakdown: 飲む (godan) → u_to_a stem 飲ま + なければ = 飲まなければ. なりません (polite) is more formal than いけない (casual).

parts.gloss: not eat + と (plain negative + conditional と)

examples.breakdown: 食べる (ichidan) → plain negative 食べない + と + いけない = ないといけない pattern. Interchangeable with 食べなければいけない.

sections.explanation: The ば conditional expresses a general 'if' — if this condition holds, then that result follows. It is hypothetical and open-ended, often used for advice, general truths, or speaker wishes. Unlike たら, the result does not need to be a completed event. Unlike と, the result is not automatic or inevitable.
notes: Formation — Godan verbs: u_to_e stem + ば: 行く→行けば, 話す→話せば, 飲む→飲めば, 帰る→帰れば.
Formation — Ichidan verbs: drop る + れば: 食べる→食べれば, 起きる→起きれば.
Formation — Irregular: する→すれば, くる→来れば, ある→あれば.
Formation — い-adjectives: drop い + ければ: 早い→早ければ, 高い→高ければ.
Use ば for general advice or open hypotheticals: 毎日 練習すれば、上手になる (if you practice every day, you'll get better). The speaker doesn't know if you will practice — it's a general condition.
ば is NOT used for one-time past events as triggers. Use たら for completed-event triggers: ✗ 試験が 終われば instead use ✓ 試験が 終わったら。

parts.gloss: speak (ば form of 話す — godan: 話せ + ば)

examples.en: If you speak every day, you'll get better.
examples.breakdown: 話す (godan) → u_to_e stem 話せ + ば = 話せば. Open hypothetical: the condition is general advice, not tied to a specific completed event.

parts.gloss: exist (ば form of ある — irregular: あれば)
let's watch (plain volitional of 見る)

examples.en: If there's time, let's watch a movie.
examples.breakdown: ある (irr_aru) → irregular ば form あれば. The condition (having time) is hypothetical — the speaker doesn't know yet if time will exist.

sections.explanation: The と conditional expresses a result that always follows automatically from the condition — a natural law, a fixed fact, or an inevitable consequence. It is used after plain-form verbs and adjectives. Unlike ば and たら, the result clause cannot express the speaker's requests, suggestions, or intentions (no volitional forms, no commands, no てください).
notes: Formation: plain form (dictionary form) verb/adjective + と. 行く→行くと, 押す→押すと, 寒い→寒いと.
Use と for: directions ('go right and the station is there'), natural laws ('spring comes and it warms up'), mechanical results ('press the button and the door opens'), and habitual truths.
と cannot be followed by volitional forms (〜よう), requests (〜てください), or suggestions. Use たら or ば for those: ✗ 終わると 行こう → ✓ 終わったら 行こう.
Contrast with たら: と describes automatic / always-true results; たら describes a one-time event that triggers the next action after completion.

parts.gloss: exit (plain form of 出る + と — automatic result)
there is (plain form of ある)

examples.en: Exit the station and there's a convenience store on the right.
examples.breakdown: 出る (ichidan) → plain form 出る + と. The result is automatic and always true — every time you exit, the store is there. Classic use of と for directions.

parts.gloss: become (plain form of なる + と — automatic result)

examples.en: When spring comes, the flowers bloom.
examples.breakdown: なる → plain form なる + と. A natural, inevitable result: every spring, flowers bloom. This kind of always-true statement is a hallmark of と.

sections.meaning: when ~ / if ~ (after the action is completed)
sections.explanation: The たら conditional is formed from the plain past (た/だ) + ら. It expresses a condition that is triggered once the preceding action or event is completed. Unlike と, the result can be a request, suggestion, or intention. Unlike ば, たら links to a specific event completing, not a general hypothetical.
notes: Formation — Godan verbs: same euphonic changes as plain past + ら: 行く→行ったら, 終わる→終わったら, 飲む→飲んだら, 話す→話したら.
Formation — Ichidan verbs: drop る + たら: 食べる→食べたら, 起きる→起きたら.
Formation — Irregular: する→したら, くる→来たら.
Formation — い-adjectives: drop い + かったら: 早い→早かったら, いい→よかったら (irregular — stem is よ-).

たら can express: (1) a sequenced trigger — 試験が 終わったら、遊ぼう (when the exam is done, let's play); (2) a discovered result — 家に 帰ったら、手紙が あった (when I got home, there was a letter); (3) a hypothetical wish — 時間が あったら、行きたい (if I had time, I'd want to go).
Unlike と, the result after たら can be volitional (〜よう, 〜ましょう) or a request (〜てください).

parts.gloss: finish (たら form of 終わる — godan: 終わった + ら)
call (te-form of 電話する — request)

examples.en: When work is done, give me a call.
examples.breakdown: 終わる (godan, -ru) → plain past 終わった + ら = 終わったら. The result (call me) is a request — possible with たら but not with と.

parts.gloss: go (たら form of 行く — godan: 行った + ら)
want to eat (plain desire form of 食べる)

examples.en: If I go to Japan, I want to eat sushi.
examples.breakdown: 行く (godan) → plain past 行った + ら = 行ったら. Here たら is hypothetical (if I were to go) with a personal wish as the result.

sections.meaning: if ~ / in that case (contextual — based on established information)
sections.explanation: なら is the contextual conditional. Unlike ば, と, and たら which introduce new conditions, なら always responds to something already in the conversation — what the other person just said, a plan they mentioned, or an assumption that's already been established. The nuance is 'given what you just told me' or 'if that's the case'.
notes: Formation: plain form verb/noun/adjective + なら. 行く→行くなら, 学生→学生なら, 上手→上手なら.
なら responds to established context. If someone says 東京に 行く, you respond: 東京に 行くなら、渋谷に 寄ってみて (if you're going to Tokyo [as you just said], try stopping by Shibuya).
Do not use なら for brand-new hypotheticals. Use ば or たら: ✗ 時間が あるなら (introducing new info) → ✓ 時間が あれば / あったら.
なら is also the ば-conditional form of な-adjectives (from conditional_ba rules for na_adj: add なら). Context usually makes the meaning clear.
Common pattern: noun + なら for topic-based conditions — 東京なら (if it's Tokyo / speaking of Tokyo). This carries a strong sense of 'given this topic'.

parts.gloss: study (plain form of 勉強する + なら — contextual)

examples.en: If you're studying Japanese, this book is good.
examples.breakdown: 勉強する → plain form + なら = 勉強するなら. なら responds to context — perhaps someone just mentioned wanting to study Japanese. The response 'this book is good' is a recommendation based on that established fact.

parts.gloss: watch (plain form of 見る + なら — contextual)

examples.en: If you're going to watch a movie (as you've mentioned), this theater is good.
examples.breakdown: 見る → plain form + なら = 見るなら. The speaker is reacting to something already said (e.g., 'I want to watch a movie'). なら picks up that context and makes a recommendation.

sections.title: Four Conditionals — ば / と / たら / なら

items.label: ば
points: Used for advice, general truths, and open-ended conditions
Result can be volitional (let's ~) or a statement
NOT used for one-time completed-event triggers

example.en: If you speak every day, you'll get better.

items.label: と
points: Automatic or inevitable result — 'when X, Y always follows'
Natural laws, directions, mechanical results, habitual facts
Result clause CANNOT be a request, suggestion, or intention
The result always follows — no exceptions

parts.text: 右に コンビニが ある。
parts.gloss: there's a convenience store on the right

example.en: Exit the station and there's a convenience store on the right.

items.label: たら
points: Works for both one-time events and hypotheticals
Result CAN be a request (〜て), suggestion (〜よう), or intention
Most versatile conditional — the safe default when unsure

parts.text: 仕事が 終わったら、
parts.gloss: when work is done (たら form)

example.en: When work is done, give me a call.

items.label: なら
points: Contextual — responds to something already established
Reacts to what the other person said or implied
Condition must already be in the conversation/context
NOT used to introduce brand-new hypotheticals

parts.text: 日本語を 勉強するなら、
parts.gloss: if you're studying Japanese (as you said)

parts.text: この 本が いい。

example.en: If you're studying Japanese (as you mentioned), this book is good.

sections.tip: Quick rule: と = always automatic (no requests); たら = after completion (most flexible); ば = general if (advice/truths); なら = reacting to context (must already be established).

sections.explanation: ば〜ほど expresses that as one thing increases, another increases proportionally. The verb appears twice — first in the ば conditional form, then again in its plain form before ほど. The result clause describes what increases or changes in proportion.
notes: Formation: [verb-ば] + [same verb plain form] + ほど + [result]. 読む→読めば 読むほど (the more you read). 勉強する→勉強すれば するほど (the more you study).
The verb repeats: ば form + plain form + ほど. Both uses of the verb refer to the same action increasing in frequency or degree.
The result clause often uses adjective adverbial forms with なる: おもしろく なる (becomes more interesting), 上手に なる (becomes more skilled).
い-adjective pattern: 早ければ 早いほど、いい (the earlier the better). The adjective appears in ければ form then its plain form.
ほど here is the degree particle (p_hodo) meaning 'to the extent of'. It marks the proportional degree to which the result follows.

parts.gloss: read (ば form of 読む — godan: 読め + ば)
read (plain form of 読む) + ほど (to that extent)
interesting (adverbial form of おもしろい)

examples.en: The more you read, the more interesting it becomes.
examples.breakdown: 読む (godan) → ば form 読めば + plain form 読む + ほど. The verb repeats to express proportional escalation. Result: おもしろく なる = becomes more interesting.

parts.gloss: study (ば form of する — irregular: すれば) — with 勉強 noun
do (plain form of する) + ほど

examples.en: The more you study, the better you understand.
examples.breakdown: 勉強する suru compound: 勉強 (noun) + する → すれば (ば form) for the condition, する (plain) + ほど for the degree marker. よく 分かる = understand well/better.

sections.title: Exam Crunch and Movie Plans (Casual)
sections.context: りきぞ and けん are chatting the day before an exam. りきぞ is stressed about studying; けん tries to be encouraging and they make plans for after the exam.

lines.jp: 明日 試験が あるから、今日は 勉強 しなければ いけないよ。
lines.en: I have an exam tomorrow, so I have to study today.

lines.jp: そうか。毎日 勉強 すれば、試験は よく 分かるよ。
lines.en: Is that so. If you study every day, you'll understand the material well.

lines.jp: 試験が 終わったら、映画を 見ようよ！
lines.en: When the exam is done, let's watch a movie!

lines.jp: 映画を 見るなら、この 映画館に 行こう！
lines.en: If you're going to watch a movie, let's go to this theater!

lines.jp: そうだね。でも、今は 勉強 しないと いけないんだ。
lines.en: You're right. But I have to study now.

sections.instructions: Choose the correct form or word to complete each sentence.

items.translation: Go right and the station is there (automatic result).
items.explanation: と expresses an automatic, always-true result: 行くと、駅が ある — every time you go right, the station is there. たら would imply 'after you've gone right, you'll find a station', which is less natural for fixed directions.

items.translation: When work is done, give me a call.
items.explanation: たら is the completed-event conditional: 終わったら = when/if work is done. と cannot be used here because the result is a request (電話して) — と does not allow request or intention forms in the result.

items.before: 薬を 飲まなければ
items.explanation: なければ いけない is the obligation construction meaning 'must' or 'have to'. 飲まなければ いけない = must drink (the medicine). いい would mean 'it's fine not to drink', which is the opposite meaning.

items.translation: The more you study, the better you understand.
items.explanation: ば〜ほど is the 'the more ~, the more ~' pattern. すれば する ほど = the more you do (study), the [more you understand]. ほど marks the proportional degree.

sections.title: Practice: Obligations & Conditionals
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.q: 映画を 見る____、この 映画館に 行こう。
items.explanation: なら responds to context already established — e.g., someone just said they want to watch a movie. 見るなら (if you're going to watch one, as you mentioned) → recommendation. と would require an automatic result; ば is for new hypotheticals; たら implies after watching.

items.q: 帰らなければ いけない。— What form of 帰る is 帰らなければ?
choices: Nakereba form (obligation stem — 帰ら + なければ)
Plain negative (帰らない without the ない)
items.answer: Nakereba form (obligation stem — 帰ら + なければ)
items.explanation: 帰る (godan) → u_to_a stem 帰ら + なければ = 帰らなければ. This is the nakereba form, used in obligation constructions なければいけない and なければなりません. The stem is identical to the plain_negative stem (帰ら), but adds なければ instead of ない.

choices: 駅を 出ると、右に コンビニが ある。
試験が 終わると、映画を 見よう。
日本語を 勉強すると、この 本が いい。
映画を 見ると、この 映画館に 行こう。
items.answer: 駅を 出ると、右に コンビニが ある。
items.explanation: と requires an automatic result that cannot be a request or intention. 駅を 出ると、コンビニが ある = automatic/always-true (the store is always there). The others fail: 終わると 見よう uses a volitional (✗ with と); 勉強すると この本が いい is a recommendation (not automatic); 見ると 行こう is a suggestion (✗ with と).

items.q: 読めば 読むほど、おもしろくなる。— What does this sentence mean?
choices: The more you read, the more interesting it becomes.
If you read, it will become interesting.
Reading is interesting, so read more.
You should read until it becomes interesting.
items.answer: The more you read, the more interesting it becomes.
items.explanation: 読めば 読むほど is the ば〜ほど pattern: the verb repeats (ば form + plain form + ほど) to express proportional escalation. おもしろくなる = becomes interesting. The whole sentence means: as reading increases, interestingness increases proportionally.

items.q: What is the key difference between ば and たら?
choices: ば is for general hypotheticals and advice; たら is for completed-event triggers and allows requests
ば is for past events; たら is for future events
ば requires a negative result; たら requires a positive result
ば is polite; たら is casual
items.answer: ば is for general hypotheticals and advice; たら is for completed-event triggers and allows requests
items.explanation: ば expresses open, general conditions (毎日 話せば、上手になる = if you speak every day, you'll improve — general advice). たら links to a specific event completing and allows requests/intentions in the result (仕事が 終わったら、電話して = when work is done, call me — specific trigger + request). たら is the most versatile conditional.
